sections.heading: Overview
sections.content: The management of small cell lung cancer (SCLC) is multimodal, with treatment intent and approach dictated by disease stage, patient performance status (PS), and the presence of specific clinical scenarios. The overarching goal for limited-stage SCLC (LS-SCLC) is curative intent using a combination of systemic therapy and thoracic radiation therapy (RT), with surgery considered for a select subset of patients [1, 2]. For extensive-stage SCLC (ES-SCLC), the goal shifts to palliation and prolongation of survival, primarily through systemic therapy, with RT used for symptom control or in select cases for consolidative benefit [1, 16]. The workup and initiation of treatment should be expedited due to the aggressive nature of the disease, with studies performed in parallel to avoid delay [1]. A multidisciplinary evaluation is strongly recommended, particularly before any surgical intervention, to ensure optimal patient selection and treatment planning [1]. Integration of palliative care early in the treatment course is also advised to manage symptoms and discuss goals of care [1].

edges.label: Other LS (stage I-III not very early)
If poor PS due to SCLC
If poor PS not due to SCLC

cards.title: Curative
cards.detail: Patient group: Limited-stage SCLC (stage I–III that can be safely treated with definitive RT) with good PS (0–2). Approach: Concurrent chemoradiation is the standard of care (category 1) [1, 98, 99]. Four cycles of platinum/etoposide-based chemotherapy are recommended, with RT initiated early (cycle 1 or 2) [1, 10]. Consolidation durvalumab is recommended for patients without progression after chemoradiation (category 1) [1, 115]. For very early-stage disease (T1–2,N0), surgery (lobectomy) followed by adjuvant systemic therapy is an option, or stereotactic ablative radiotherapy (SABR) followed by systemic therapy for medically inoperable patients [1, 13, 14]. Prophylactic cranial irradiation (PCI) should be considered for responders, though its benefit is less clear in pathologic stage I [1, 42–44].

cards.title: Palliative
cards.detail: Patient group: Extensive-stage SCLC or limited-stage disease with poor PS (3–4) not due to SCLC. Approach: Systemic therapy with or without RT to symptomatic sites is the primary treatment [1, 16]. Preferred regimens combine platinum/etoposide with an immune checkpoint inhibitor (atezolizumab or durvalumab) [1, 118–120]. For patients with poor PS due to SCLC, systemic therapy may still be considered, but for poor PS not due to SCLC, individualized therapy including supportive care is recommended [1]. RT is used for palliation of symptomatic metastases (e.g., bone, brain, spinal cord compression) [1, 302–304].

cards.title: Subsequent/Palliative
cards.detail: Patient group: Relapsed or refractory SCLC. Approach: Treatment is based on clinical expertise and trial data, without strict adherence to chemotherapy-free interval categories [1]. Preferred options include tarlatamab-dlle, clinical trial enrollment, irinotecan, lurbinectedin (if not used previously), platinum-based rechallenge for sensitive relapse, or topotecan [1]. Nivolumab or pembrolizumab are options if not previously treated with an ICI [1]. Dose reduction or growth factor support should be considered for patients with PS 2 [1].
